title: How to tell how deep your needle is
filename: How to tell how deep your needle is.en.vtt
excerpt: If you want to be in the hypodermis um then when you lift you should just see all the tissue lift but not a clear line of blanching in most of the face so how are we supposed to know how deep we are when we can't see so depth check is the most important thing you can do so um now…

segments.text: if you want to be in the hypodermis um then when you lift you should just see all the tissue lift but not a clear line of blanching in most of the face so how are we supposed to know how deep we are when we can't see so depth check is the most important thing you can do so um now you've got to know a little bit about the type of skin you're injecting so there's big differences in the thickness of the skin if you're injecting the tear trough firstly there are fewer layers of three layers in the teardrop for example but also it's only 0.2 millimeters thick the dermis so it can look like you're very superficial but you are still under the dermis you're not in the epidermis for example even though you're so superficial because the skin is thinner so you need to know the skin and there's different thicknesses so chins are very thick the bella is a very thick place to inject and that might change what you see but essentially it's about depth check and that blanching thing which i described earlier when you see that you see it blanche if you want to be in the hypodermis um

hypodermis um then when you lift you should just see all the tissue lift but not a clear line of blanching in most of the face so typical would be you know a crease on your cheek for example and if you want to be inflating the volume of a superficial cheek fat pad a little bit and when you lift up you shouldn't be clear where the needle is you should just see the shape of the needle and maybe slight blanching but it shouldn't be clear at all when you're more superficial much clearer where the needle is and where the blanching is and if you're too superficial there's continuous blanching it doesn't change at all and no matter what you do with your needle it's white but it's also a very small area of white just basically following the shape of the needle and of course if you're on the periosteum you know that because you can feel it it's hard

transcript: if you want to be in the hypodermis um then when you lift you should just see all the tissue lift but not a clear line of blanching in most of the face so how are we supposed to know how deep we are when we can't see so depth check is the most important thing you can do so um now you've got to know a little bit about the type of skin you're injecting so there's big differences in the thickness of the skin if you're injecting the tear trough firstly there are fewer layers of three layers in the teardrop for example but also it's only 0.2 millimeters thick the dermis so it can look like you're very superficial but you are still under the dermis you're not in the epidermis for example even though you're so superficial because the skin is thinner so you need to know the skin and there's different thicknesses so chins are very thick the bella is a very thick place to inject and that might change what you see but essentially it's about depth check and that blanching thing which i described earlier when you see that you see it blanche if you want to be in the hypodermis um hypodermis um then when you lift you should just see all the tissue lift but not a clear line of blanching in most of the face so typical would be you know a crease on your cheek for example and if you want to be inflating the volume of a superficial cheek fat pad a little bit and when you lift up you shouldn't be clear where the needle is you should just see the shape of the needle and maybe slight blanching but it shouldn't be clear at all when you're more superficial much clearer where the needle is and where the blanching is and if you're too superficial there's continuous blanching it doesn't change at all and no matter what you do with your needle it's white but it's also a very small area of white just basically following the shape of the needle and of course if you're on the periosteum you know that because you can feel it it's hard
